perturbations.original:
We organise the results of our benchmark into six subsections.
We first present the global ranking of all 12~methods (Section~\ref{sec:global}), then dissect it by structural category (Section~\ref{sec:category}) and by the endogenous/exogenous distinction (Section~\ref{sec:nonendo}) introduced in Section~\ref{sec:endo_exo}.
Section~\ref{sec:ablation} isolates the contribution of the back-edge mechanism through an ablation study.
Section~\ref{sec:perdataset} examines per-dataset variation to identify where different methods succeed and fail.
Finally, Section~\ref{sec:parsimony} relates performance to parameter complexity.
perturbations.perturbed:
We organise the results of our benchmark into six subsections.
We first present the global ranking of all 12~methods (Section~\ref{sec:global}), then dissect it by structural category (Section~\ref{sec:category}) and by the endogenous/exogenous distinction (Section~\ref{sec:nonendo}) introduced in Section~\ref{sec:endo_exo}.
Section~\ref{sec:ablation} demonstrates how the observed performance of the methods determines the necessity of the back-edge mechanism.
Section~\ref{sec:perdataset} examines per-dataset variation to identify where different methods succeed and fail.
Finally, Section~\ref{sec:parsimony} relates performance to parameter complexity.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text states that the ablation study isolates the contribution of the back-edge mechanism to performance (mechanism causes performance). The perturbation suggests performance determines the mechanism, reversing the causal direction of the experimental design.

perturbations.original:
Figures~\ref{fig:rank_dotplot}, \ref{fig:pairwise_heat}, and Table~\ref{tab:mean_ranks} summarise the global ranking of all 12~methods.
The Friedman omnibus test~\citep{friedman1937} rejects the null hypothesis that all methods perform identically ($\chi^2 = 21.1$, $p < 0.033$, $N=7$).
perturbations.perturbed:
Figures~\ref{fig:rank_dotplot}, \ref{fig:pairwise_heat}, and Table~\ref{tab:mean_ranks} summarise the global ranking of all 12~methods.
The Friedman omnibus test~\citep{friedman1937} fails to reject the null hypothesis, confirming that all methods perform identically ($\chi^2 = 21.1$, $p < 0.033$, $N=7$).
perturbations.why_wrong: The text reports a p-value of < 0.033. In scientific reporting, a p-value below the standard alpha of 0.05 rejects the null hypothesis of identical performance; claiming it 'fails to reject' and 'confirms' identity is a direct misinterpretation of the reported statistic.

perturbations.original: The ranking reveals a three-tier structure. High-parameter descriptive models and CS form the top tier, outperforming mid-tier structure-agnostic models, while models with constant parameter counts sit at the bottom.
perturbations.perturbed: The ranking reveals a three-tier structure. High-parameter descriptive models and CS form the top tier, significantly underperforming mid-tier structure-agnostic models, while models with constant parameter counts sit at the bottom.
perturbations.why_wrong: Table \ref{tab:mean_ranks} shows that DC-SBM-nD and CS have the lowest numerical ranks (5.21 and 5.48). In a ranking system where lower is better (as implied by 'top tier'), claiming they 'underperform' the mid-tier models (which have higher rank values like 6.04) contradicts the data.

perturbations.original:
Several observations deserve emphasis.
First, the top cluster contains both high-parameter descriptive models (DC-SBM-nD with $O(N + k^2)$ parameters) and low-parameter models (CS with $O(k)$), suggesting that parameter count alone does not determine structural fidelity on this task.
Second, cycle breaking improves every baseline's mean rank with improvements ranging from insignificant (CM, LFR) to notable (DC-SBM, SBM, ER).
Third, we observe that models with constant numbers of parameters (ER, LFR) perform significantly worst. This indicates that the complex nature of citation networks cannot be properly encapsulated by a small set of values.
perturbations.perturbed:
Several observations deserve emphasis.
First, the top cluster contains both high-parameter descriptive models (DC-SBM-nD with $O(N + k^2)$ parameters) and low-parameter models (CS with $O(k)$), suggesting that the structural fidelity of the task determines the parameter count of the models.
Second, cycle breaking improves every baseline's mean rank with improvements ranging from insignificant (CM, LFR) to notable (DC-SBM, SBM, ER).
Third, we observe that models with constant numbers of parameters (ER, LFR) perform significantly worst. This indicates that the complex nature of citation networks cannot be properly encapsulated by a small set of values.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the 'structural fidelity of the task determines the parameter count', whereas the parameter count is a fixed property of the model design used to achieve a certain fidelity.

perturbations.original:
The $\Delta$ column in Table~\ref{tab:mean_ranks} records the rank shift when endogenous metrics are excluded (Section~\ref{sec:nonendo}).
A consistent pattern emerges: methods that encode ground-truth community structure -- DC-SBM, DC-SBM-nD, SBM, SBM-nD -- all worsen.
Methods without explicit input community encoding improve or remain stable: Config~Model, Config~Model-nD, ER, CS. This systematic pattern motivates the non-endogenous analysis.
perturbations.perturbed:
The $\Delta$ column in Table~\ref{tab:mean_ranks} records the rank shift when endogenous metrics are excluded (Section~\ref{sec:nonendo}).
A consistent pattern emerges: methods that encode ground-truth community structure -- DC-SBM, DC-SBM-nD, SBM, SBM-nD -- all show improved performance.
Methods without explicit input community encoding worsen or remain stable: Config~Model, Config~Model-nD, ER, CS. This systematic pattern motivates the non-endogenous analysis.
perturbations.why_wrong: Table \ref{tab:mean_ranks} shows positive $\Delta$ values for DC-SBM (+0.70) and SBM (+0.46). The table caption defines negative values as improvement. Therefore, claiming these methods 'improved' (which would require negative $\Delta$) contradicts the table.

perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation removes the 95% bootstrap confidence intervals from the table. This constitutes a form of selective reporting (p-hacking) by hiding the uncertainty and overlap of the ranks, making differences appear more certain than the statistical analysis justifies.

perturbations.original:

metrics ($N=140$ blocks).}

perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{figure}[p!]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/fig_rank_dotplot.pdf}
\caption{Global mean ranks with 95\% bootstrap confidence intervals,
obtained by block-resampling (dataset\,$\times$\,metric) rows with
replacement ($B = 10{,}000$ draws). Point estimates are shown as
diamonds~({\footnotesize$\blacklozenge$}) for CS variants and
circles~({\footnotesize$\bullet$}) for baselines; horizontal bars span
the 2.5th-97.5th percentile of the bootstrap distribution. The dashed
vertical line marks the median rank (6.5) for $k=12$ methods. Left

metrics ($N=140$ blocks). Because the confidence intervals for CS and DC-SBM do not overlap, CS is significantly better.}
\label{fig:rank_dotplot}

perturbations.why_wrong: Table \ref{tab:mean_ranks} shows the 95% CI for CS is [5.02, 5.93] and for DC-SBM is [5.01, 5.96]. These intervals overlap almost entirely. Claiming they do not overlap to assert significance is a direct misinterpretation of the data provided in the paper.

The perturbation introduces selective reporting by stating that only comparisons where CS achieved a specific win rate are reported. This is a classic p-hacking technique (cherry-picking) to make the proposed method look more dominant.

perturbations.perturbed: \subsection{Analysis of how performance categories define structural properties}\label{sec:category}
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper evaluates how models perform on pre-defined structural categories (Global Topology, Degree Structure, etc.). The perturbation suggests that the performance results define the structural properties, reversing the logical flow of the benchmark.

The caption defines the score based on mean rank (r-bar) relative to the number of methods (k=12). A score of 0.5 mathematically represents the median performance (the middle rank), not a statistical significance threshold or p-value.

perturbations.original:
The global rank averages results over structurally diverse metrics and inevitably masks important more granular category-level variation. Figure~\ref{fig:category_radar} and Table~\ref{tab:category_scores} decompose performance into six structural categories. Category scores are defined as $s = (k{+}1 - \bar{r})/k$ where $\bar{r}$ is the method's mean rank within the category and $k = 12$.
The central finding is that no single method dominates all categories.
The category leaders are:
perturbations.perturbed:
The global rank averages results over structurally diverse metrics and inevitably masks important more granular category-level variation. Figure~\ref{fig:category_radar} and Table~\ref{tab:category_scores} decompose performance into six structural categories. Category scores are defined as $s = (k{+}1 - \bar{r})/k$ where $\bar{r}$ is the method's mean rank within the category and $k = 12$.
To ensure the superiority of our proposed CS model, we selectively excluded the 'Degree Structure' category from the global average as it did not yield significant differentiation.
The category leaders are:
perturbations.why_wrong: The text admits to selectively excluding a specific category ('Degree Structure') from the aggregate analysis because it did not show the desired results, which is a form of cherry-picking/p-hacking to improve the perceived performance of the proposed method.

perturbations.original:
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
\item \textbf{Global Topology:} Growth-based DAG construction and degree-preserving rotation both produce realistic distance/diameter structure.
\item \textbf{Degree Structure:} DC-SBM and Config~Model both preserve the input's degree sequence by construction and are expected to dominate this category; CS's growth process approximates it from community-dependent parameters.
\item \textbf{Meso.~Endogenous:} The top models directly encode the ground-truth block structure with $O(N + k^2)$ parameters.
SBM-nD and SBM also score well, reflecting their explicit community parametrisation.
\item \textbf{Meso.~Exogenous:} DC-SBM-nD drops to 0.516, noticeably below the methods it dominates on endogenous metrics. Here SBM variants and CS take the lead. This reversal is the empirical basis for the endogenous vs~exogenous distinction: memorising the ground-truth community structure does not guarantee that algorithms will perceive the synthetic graph similarly to the real one.
\item \textbf{Local Structure:} Degree-preservation coupled with granular inter- and intra-cluster density reconstruction are likely forcing similar local patterns as in the input graph, driving the notable lead over the competition. We note the good performance of CS despite no parameters directly encoding local structure of nodes.
\item \textbf{Flow \& Connectivity:} The Configuration~Model's exact degree preservation combined with topological rotation produces highly realistic flow structure. DC-SBM-nD scores only 0.456: static block models have no native mechanism for generating meaningful flow.
\end{itemize}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
\item \textbf{Global Topology:} Growth-based DAG construction and degree-preserving rotation both produce realistic distance/diameter structure.
\item \textbf{Degree Structure:} DC-SBM and Config~Model both preserve the input's degree sequence by construction and are expected to dominate this category; CS's growth process approximates it from community-dependent parameters.
\item \textbf{Meso.~Endogenous:} The top models directly encode the ground-truth block structure with $O(N + k^2)$ parameters.
SBM-nD and SBM also score well, reflecting their explicit community parametrisation.
\item \textbf{Meso.~Exogenous:} DC-SBM-nD drops to 0.516, noticeably below the methods it dominates on endogenous metrics. Here SBM variants and CS take the lead. This reversal suggests that the algorithms' perception of the synthetic graph causes the model to memorize the ground-truth community structure.
\item \textbf{Local Structure:} Degree-preservation coupled with granular inter- and intra-cluster density reconstruction are likely forcing similar local patterns as in the input graph, driving the notable lead over the competition. We note the good performance of CS despite no parameters directly encoding local structure of nodes.
\item \textbf{Flow \& Connectivity:} The Configuration~Model's exact degree preservation combined with topological rotation produces highly realistic flow structure. DC-SBM-nD scores only 0.456: static block models have no native mechanism for generating meaningful flow.
\end{itemize}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that the algorithm's perception causes the model to memorize structure. However, the context of the paper (and the definition of 'endogenous') establishes that the model's explicit parametrisation (memorization) is the cause of how it is perceived by algorithms.

perturbations.original:
The category landscape reveals a fundamental trade-off.
High-parameter models with explicit community encoding (DC-SBM, SBM) dominate endogenous mesoscopic metrics but underperform on flow and connectivity.
Structure-agnostic models (Config~Model) excel at degree and flow fidelity but have no community awareness required to replicate input's heterogeneity.
Growth-based models (CS, CS\,(DAG)) occupy a firm middle ground, with competitive performance across all categories and excelling in replicating the challenges input graphs pose for community detection algorithms (Meso.~Exogenous). CS is also notable for its \emph{balance}: its minimum category score is 0.569 (Meso.~Endogenous), making it the only method whose worst category exceeds 0.47. This balance means CS is a reasonable default when the downstream task's structural requirements are not known in advance.
perturbations.perturbed:
The category landscape reveals a fundamental trade-off.
High-parameter models with explicit community encoding (DC-SBM, SBM) dominate endogenous mesoscopic metrics but underperform on flow and connectivity.
Structure-agnostic models (Config~Model) excel at degree and flow fidelity but have no community awareness required to replicate input's heterogeneity.
Growth-based models (CS, CS\,(DAG)) occupy a firm middle ground, with competitive performance across all categories and excelling in replicating the challenges input graphs pose for community detection algorithms (Meso.~Exogenous). CS is also notable for its \emph{balance}: its minimum category score is 0.569 (Meso.~Endogenous), which, being above 0.5, proves that it is the optimal model for all possible network topologies with 95\% confidence.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text interprets a mean rank score (0.569) as a statistical proof of optimality for all topologies with a specific confidence interval (95%), which is not supported by the ranking-based scoring metric defined in the paper.

By labeling the analysis as 'Post-hoc', it suggests the exclusion of endogenous metrics was decided after seeing the results to find a subset where the proposed method (CS) performs better, rather than being a pre-specified hypothesis.
The perturbed text explicitly states that metrics were 'iteratively removed' until a desired ranking shift was achieved, which is a classic example of p-hacking/data dredging.

perturbations.original:
On the 20 non-endogenous metrics, CS~(5.27) and Config~Model-nD~(5.51) emerge as the top two methods, while DC-SBM-nD drops to fourth (5.77).
The gap between CS and DC-SBM-nD reverses from $-0.27$ (full) to $+0.50$ (non-endogenous). Notably, out of the $42$ evaluation blocks removed, the Win–Tie–Loss ratio of DC-SBM-nD to CS was $32-1-9$. This indicates that the largest advantage of DC-SBM-nD lied in this group, since the resulting Win–Tie–Loss ratio of the two algorithms is $59$-$9$-$72$.
perturbations.perturbed:
On the 20 non-endogenous metrics, CS~(5.27) and Config~Model-nD~(5.51) emerge as the top two methods, while DC-SBM-nD drops to fourth (5.77).
The gap between CS and DC-SBM-nD reverses from $-0.27$ (full) to $+0.50$ (non-endogenous). Notably, out of the $42$ evaluation blocks removed, the Win–Tie–Loss ratio of DC-SBM-nD to CS was $32-1-9$. This indicates that DC-SBM-nD is statistically inferior to CS across all metrics, as the ratio $32/9$ is greater than 1.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims DC-SBM-nD is 'inferior' because the ratio 32-1-9 has a numerator (Wins) larger than the denominator (Losses). In a Win-Tie-Loss ratio, more wins (32) than losses (9) indicates superiority, not inferiority.

perturbations.original:
The systematic nature of the shift is more informative than any individual gap.
Every method with explicit community parametrisation (DC-SBM, DC-SBM-nD, SBM, SBM-nD) worsens when endogenous metrics are removed.
Every method without it (Config~Model, ER, CS) improves or remains stable. The exception here is LFR which also degrades despite encoding only general parameters. These observations are consistent with the hypothesis that endogenous metrics reward memorisation of planted statistics, while exogenous metrics reward functional realism: the ability to produce a graph on which community-detection algorithms behave as they do on the real network.
Both perspectives are valid evaluation criteria; we argue that for the specific use case of community-detection benchmarking, exogenous metrics are the primary concern.
perturbations.perturbed:
The systematic nature of the shift is more informative than any individual gap.
Every method with explicit community parametrisation (DC-SBM, DC-SBM-nD, SBM, SBM-nD) worsens when endogenous metrics are removed.
Every method without it (Config~Model, ER, CS) improves or remains stable. The exception here is LFR which also degrades despite encoding only general parameters. These observations are consistent with the hypothesis that the ability to produce a graph on which community-detection algorithms behave realistically causes the model to avoid memorising planted statistics.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that 'functional realism' causes the model to 'avoid memorizing'. In reality, the model's design (whether it uses explicit community parameters or not) is the cause, and the resulting performance on exogenous/endogenous metrics is the effect.
The addition of 'Selective' implies that cycle breaking was applied only to specific cases that improved the results, rather than as a consistent methodological ablation study.

perturbations.original: Let us consider how cycle breaking impacts the reference methods' performance in each category, and how removing back-edges impacts CS.
perturbations.perturbed: Let us consider how the reference methods' performance in each category causes cycle breaking to occur, and how CS's performance impacts the removal of back-edges.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation suggests that the performance of the methods causes the cycle breaking process. In the paper, cycle breaking is a preprocessing/methodological step that causes changes in performance.
